logistics.lodging_strategy: Limited availability considered in scheduling; book early due to regional events and post-emergency recovery.[1]

final_verdict.one_liner: New Zealand's iconic multi-day road stage race, toughest in the south with dual 2026 editions.

videos.title: 2015 SBS Bank Tour of Southland - Stage 4 Highlights
videos.description: Race highlights from Stage 4 of the 2015 SBS Bank Tour of Southland from Mossburn to Coronet Peak, Queenstown with commentators Julian Ineson and Doug Bath. For more information visit www.tourofsouthland.com.
videos.transcript:
[Music]
[Applause]
Welcome to Invagle in New Zealand's deep
south, home of cycling and a region rich
in scenery and character. For the past
59 years, Envagle has opened its doors
to a small army of elite cycling teams
who descend on the region for one of the
biggest and toughest races on the New
Zealand cycling
calendar. Across seven stages and 900
km, the cyclist will face everything the
roads, terrain, and weather of Southland
can throw at them. At the end of the
week, the best will take home the titles
and the jerseys money can't buy. This is
the 2015 SPS Bank Tour of
Southland. The tour set its sights on
Tano on Wednesday. Riverton and the
Southern Scenic Group turned on a
picture postcard day, but the Pelaton
had little time to take it in with a tax
on from the start. A breakaway of five
established within the first 10 km
featuring New Zealand champion Joe
Cooper, track team pursuit world
champion Alex Frame, Logan Griffin, Mike
and Matt Zenovich.
The leaders pushed out to over 4 minutes
as Cooper led them over the famed
Blackmount Hill Climb. And it was there
that the move from the Palaton finally
came. Tour leader Brad Evans, his
nearest rival Robbie Hucker, Mike
and Tom Davidson broke free on the
descent and closed down the leaders as
they reached Manapori. The leading nine
then drove the pace over the final 20 km
with Davidson leading out the sprint
only to be run down by Alex Frame over
the final few meters.
Evans safely rolled across the line at 2
seconds to maintain his overall lead
ahead of today's defining stage to the
top of Coronet Peak in
Queenstown. Um it's pretty tough. It's
lived up to its all expectations. Tough
weather, tough roads, but um I've liked
it. I rode with Katonus last year at
Charter Mason and um got in touch with
my coach and he mentioned Robbie was
wasn't doing much at this time of year.
So um we got them over
and I told him told him how tough it
could be and and I think it's it's
definitely lived up to that early on
this year and um but you know we got
through those days and we're in a pretty
good position to give it a bit of
jandle, mate, and see what comes of it.
I don't mind it uh having to race with
the yellow. Um 17 seconds. It's not not
too much and a tough climb up coronet. I
think I'll um I should be all right. I
think I should have a good day today.
Now Joe Cooper, we saw him off the front
yesterday and he picked up quite a few
points. So it's going to be a big battle
between the two of you. Yeah. Well, he
was obviously super strong yesterday. Um
I was trying to find the the early move
and he ended up rocking off up the road
with a bunch of other guys and I think
he probably took nearly all the points
out on the course. So it might be a bit
of a fight for the next couple of days.
I think we might see a break potentially
stay to the finish this year cuz
normally it's such a hard slog all the
way up. But um it also makes it easier
for teams in yellow to defend as well
cuz normally it's you know a long old
day biggest stage of the tour. But um
either way it's still heavy roads all
the way up. So everyone will still get
to the bottom of the tide legs. I think
everyone gets on well and has have a
laugh and things like that. So yeah, I
think it's about survival for us today.
and um pray for some wind tomorrow.
After a long day into Tiano yesterday,
the riders now have to prepare for the
Queen stage into Coronet. A change in
the route this year sees them starting
from Mosburn. So, a shorter stage which
could open up things here for a lot of
the riders to assist with the recovery
from that big day yesterday. But of
course, the climb at the end of the day,
8 km of switchbacks to the top. We have
seen some of the major players come to
the four in previous years and this year
should see no difference in the
proceedings. The Queenstown stage
provides the riders with some of the
most picturesque terrain they'll ride
over. The last climb up Coronet Peak is
going to provide the pivotal points for
the overall lead and can define and make
or break your race depending on how you
get up it.
Day five here of this SPS banker tour of
South and with a shortened stage this
year. Not starting in the traditional
Tiana but out of Mosburn for a field of
79 from the original 84
starters. Can't say that being a
shortened stage Julian is going to be
any encouragement in the minds of a lot
of the riders here today. This is day
five. It's an endurance contest. Stage
racing is just as grueling as it gets in
sport. And there'll be some seriously
tired legs here, but it never ever seems
to diminish the enthusiasm they have at
the start for trying to get away on
those early breaks and get up the road.
It's arguably the most scenic of all the
stages here in the tour this year. And
of course going to be finishing up at
the top of the Coronet Peak, the ski
field, but it's a few hours yet before
we see who's going to take the victory
for that. And it's very tight at the top
of the standings, Doug. only 17 seconds
separating the top two with Brad Evans
of Deneden and Hucker of Australia.
Exciting race this year. This is one of
the more uh closer races we've had for a
long time yet. It seems to be that every
day there's been a break, get out and
take time gaps which we've not yet seen
in a lot of years in the in the SPS tour
of Southland. It's quite incredible
really when you see groups go up the
road by five, six, seven, eight minutes
and yet still manage to somehow get
caught or very close to. It's very very
important though at the start of these
races that if you've got a team strategy
that you've worked out that the riders
stick to it cuz if it gets a bit
haphazard and the wrong rider gets in
the brake, it can really really ruin any
of the team's chances on that GC.
The familiar figure here of Matt
Zenovich of Imbagle going off the front
once again. And he's got the man, it's
George Atkins by the looks of it, of
Mike Greer Holmes. He's trying to get
onto his wheel. He'll know this is the
sort of guy he needs to tag on to cuz
he's always sniffing out the victory.
Matt Zenovich's got a target on his
back. The most competitive combative
rider jersey, the
stuff.co.nz combative rider jersey is
seriously on the right shoulders here. I
just love Matt Zenovich's style. He's
riding the kind of race that we used to
see from the likes of Gordon McCauley in
the old days off the front each day. I
actually believe if Matt sticks at this
long enough, he could potentially go
quite a long way to try and win this
race in years to come. He's just got
such an aggressive bike riding style,
Julian, hasn't he? He has indeed. But
unfortunately, this time round, the
field has dragged them back into it. So,
he's back into the fold as they approach
one of the early hill climbs of the day.
And it's Marcott of America that's been
placed onto the front. Taylor Gunman, a
former stage winner in this particular
stage as they make their way up towards
the Jollies
Hill. Quite a lot of lumps and bumps.
This Jolly's Hill can be quite a a tough
climb if the wind's blowing the wrong
way. But you can see here the pelatons
altogether, king of the mountains once
again coming to the front of the race
here. He's wanting to collect those ever
so important points. And who's in behind
him? Joe Cooper, he's also seeking that
jersey and he looks really strong going
over the climbs. It's going to be a
fascinating stage to see whether the
riders stay together to the base of the
Coronet Peak and then jewel it out for
the stage win and the overall lead of
the race or there's some breaks go away.
You can see here Joe Cooper, he's
another rider. He's constantly on the
look for a move. He's an opportunist
kind of guy and he's won a lot of big
bike races for that. Yeah, Nick Miller
suggested from the start of the day that
there could be a tough battle between
the two of them. With Cooper getting
that win, that secures only a one-point
difference between the two of them as we
see the veing descending skills of these
riders as they tuck on down one of the
American riders making their way down
towards
Ethel. An attack once again going off
the front here out of the Athel region
here. And it is Zenovich once again. He
just hasn't had enough. He's loving
being in that combative jersey here. And
he's taken a couple of riders with him
this time round. And it's a fellow local
rider, Luke McFersonen. Here comes the
American champion descending very
strongly indeed to make the connection
with them. That would be Marott. And we
look back to the reaction in the field,
Doug. And they're sitting up.
This is quite a a incred an an important
split in the race here, Julian Marcott,
as we've seen on day one down to Bluff.
He was at the front. Obviously, we've
talked about Matt Zenovich, Luke
McFersonen, one of the local lads, the
Barry Stewart Builder Squad. Great to
see these local guys in their local race
giving it heaps going off the front and
having a go. I really rate Luke
McFersonen. He's done a lot of hard
work. He started the sport late. Only
started when I think he was about 28
years old. But this boy's got some
talent. He's got some horsepower. He's
the kind of guy you really want to have
in a breakaway. And joining the quartet
is the Oliver's Real Food Racing once
again. This time it is Nick Reddish, one
of the under23 riders. So that will send
a ripple back in the palaton for the
pink jersey as these four now set their
straps forward here and they go to 1
minute and 10 second gap. So they start
to make a bit of a serious move on this
main
field. Some quite nice long straights
too, Julian, just out of Athel. You can
see here back in the palaton we've got
Robert Husman and the power net boys
there controlling the front. Brad Evans
having a bit of a yarn just having a
discussion about where things are at,
how they're going to play it out. But
Brad Evans is riding a very heady race
too. He hasn't panicked and uh this is
the sort of thing that he would like.
Get a breakaway up the road control
things in the pelaton wait for later on
in the stage. We saw a quick shot at the
very front of the palaton from Palonet
was Eko of Spain. One of the Spanish
riders in this year's tour, one of eight
nations represented as we make our way
through the devil staircase and
descending down with the picturesque
Lake Waka Tippu. Fantastic scenery, an
amazing part here of the central target
region. For a lot of these riders, this
will probably be the first time they've
ever been into the central ridge. You
can see here now we're coming up to the
halfway bay climb. This is the uh start
of that first climb after the devil
staircase. Luke McFersonen here is just
sitting on the back of Matthew Zenovich.
These two will have done a fair bit of
racing in the local stuff together.
Although Matt I know has had a big year
overseas. This is the crest of the
climb. There's a wee bit over the top
and Luke McFersonen's having a crack for
the points. There'll be a particular
reason for that. McFersonen of course is
in the team with the king of the
mountain. So he'll be trying to steal
some points back to protect his man that
has the KM hiding back in the pelaton.
As we see Reddish has called it a day
here. He's been away for a fair way into
this stage, but has decided it's time to
head back to that
palaton. So these riders make their way
down towards Frankton after going past
the glacial lake of Lake Wakatip and we
are down to three riders. Marott of
America, McFersonen and Zenovich. These
two now are these three I should say are
really starting to seriously consider if
they can hold off the pelaton at 335.
It's still very tight. The rains being
kept tight by that palaton controlling
things. You can see the summit of
Coronet Peak ahead in the distance.
That'll be something looming as a beacon
for all of these riders. Some of them
will be relishing it. Some of them will
be quite nervous and anxious to see
whether they not they've got the form to
climb and complete this coronet peak and
actually have an effect on their overall
standings. So you can see the pelatons
still quite well together. There's the
time split coming in from the head
commisser on the motorbike and the three
out the front. They've still got a lot
of work to do and there's still quite a
lot of lumps left before they even hit
the coronet peak. They make their way to
some huge developments here and of
course MITER 10 mega here in is also
getting developed on our right hand side
and they are the fantastic sponsors of
today's stage but the riders having to
deal with a lot of that road works as
they make their way through and we see
back in the main pelaton it is power net
no surprise there they've been given the
task of being on the front but also
interesting to note Fraser Goff of team
advantage of course they are riders in
that team that have actually taken the
stage out before so they're sniffing
around for that victory as
The last righthander before they start
the climb of Coronet Peak. Tough climb,
exceptionally tight at at the start.
There's a really, really steep gradient.
They'll be searching for the low gears
here right at the start through this
tree area. Nice and sheltered, but as
this climb goes on, it settles off a
bit. If you go too quick at the start of
this climb, it can upset your rhythm.
You can see Matt Zenovich out of the
seat. He's getting stuck in. Sometimes
you've got to meteorate your efforts on
this climb and pace yourself right from
the get- go. Yes, you're just a tad
under 10% gradient for this first 2 and
1/2 km of it. And later on, she'll kick
into over 10% gradient for for this
particular climb. As legendary local
cyclist Colin Horton would say, you
can't win a hill at the start, but you
can certainly lose it as these men grind
their way up. Look at the faces of
McFersonen. It's been a huge day out
there for them, Doug. And there's
fantastic conditions for them. But it
looks like Zenovich has done his dash
here for the day. Two days out in front
and it's starting to hit the body. The
contrasting styles of these two lead
riders. Luke McFersonen's pushing quite
a big gear and the American rider Eric

doesn't matter how you get there,

Yes, they'll be trying to set up Michael

he's got some good climbing legs on him

He will have ridden this climb a dozens

with the head buried. Marott of America.
He is absolutely dead and gone. and he's

McFersonen won't give up the ghost.
He'll try as hard as he possibly can. Of
course, he has the teammate that is

trying to keep stretching things out,
allowing his teammate to sit on the
likes of Brad Evans there with that

saw this last year from Mitchell

but I don't think it's going to matter

Michael once again. But three

McFersonen. is a man trying to gain

He knows these are two men that could

And this is gutsy stuff. This guy's been
out in the front of this race virtually

he is getting caught at the front of the

of the whole race really. And here he is

The mouth is wide open, gasping for air.
It's been a valiant effort by the young

he's just staying cool, calm, and

of course, who hold the man in second

all the way to the

trying to soften things up for his

of the Avanti Racing team. As we

Follow the 2015 SPS Bank Tour of
Southland all week with full results,
news updates, photo galleries, and video
coverage every day at tourofland.com.
videos.curation_reason: Official race highlights with detailed course commentary and rider interviews, showcasing the challenging terrain including the famed Blackmount Hill Climb.

videos.title: SBS Tour of Southland highlights stage 2 - 1 NOV 2016
videos.description:
Rotorua’s Sam Phipps achieved a career highlight after winning stage two of the 2016 SBS Bank Tour of Southland while Alex Frame finishing second took the tour leaders’ yellow jersey from his Kia Motors-Ascot Park Hotel team mate Travis McCabe.

Music credit: Adventures by A Himitsu 
Production Kinetic Media
Producer Ingrid Larson 

For more info see http://nzroadcyclist.co.nz/phipps-marks-career-highlight-on-tour-of-southland

quotes.text: Across seven stages and 900 km, the cyclist will face everything the roads, terrain, and weather of Southland can throw at them. It's pretty tough. It's lived up to all expectations. Tough weather, tough roads.
The leaders pushed out to over 4 minutes as Cooper led them over the famed Blackmount Hill Climb. And it was there that the move from the Peloton finally came.

key_challenges.name: Blackmount Hill Climb
key_challenges.description: Featured prominently early in the stage where New Zealand champion Joe Cooper led over the top, triggering attacks from the peloton on the descent.

key_challenges.name: Coronet Peak
key_challenges.description: The Queen stage climb described as 8 km of switchbacks to the top, providing pivotal points for the overall classification. Riders say it's normally such a hard slog all the way up that everyone will get to the bottom with tired legs.

terrain_notes.text: Described by riders as tough roads with heavy roads all the way up to major climbs. The terrain throws everything at riders including challenging road conditions.

race_day_tips.text: Stay close to experienced riders to avoid trouble, as mentioned by Olympic gold medalist Hamish Bond about staying near veteran Mike Tool Club.
For points jersey contenders, focus on picking up a few points along the road while maintaining your lead buffer rather than going all-out.

additional_quotes.text: It's lived up to its all expectations. Tough weather, tough roads, but I've liked it. I told him how tough it could be and it's definitely lived up to that early on this year.
I think it's about survival for us today and pray for some wind tomorrow.

rider_intel.search_text: The SBS Bank Tour of Southland spans seven stages and 900 km across New Zealand's deep south. Riders describe it as one of the toughest races on the New Zealand cycling calendar with challenging weather and tough roads throughout. Key climbs include Blackmount Hill where early breakaways typically form, and the Queen stage finale up Coronet Peak featuring 8 km of switchbacks. The Coronet climb is described as a hard slog that leaves everyone with tired legs by the bottom. Route changes include a shorter stage starting from Mosburn to aid recovery. The race features picturesque terrain through Southland with attacks typically coming on descents after major climbs. Riders emphasize survival tactics and staying close to experienced teammates to avoid trouble in the challenging conditions.
